ECO10005-Economics for Business Decision
Making

Topic 7: GDP, Inequality and Technological
Innovations


  Professor Abbas Valadkhani

  Week 7











  THE SCOPE OF MACROECONOMICS


   The four major macroeconomic issues are:
    –  economic growth
    –  unemployment
    –  inflation
       and
    –  the balance of payments current account position.

WHY DO WE STUDY NATIONAL ACCOUNTS DATA?

  For the following significant reasons:

1.To assess and measure the performance of the
     economy

2.To gauge the health of the economy

3.To uncover any underlying problems in the economy

4.To plot the long-run course of the economy over time

5.   To provide the foundation for public policies.






         GROSS DOMESTIC PRODUCT

 GDP is the total         market value         of all final    goods
   and services        produced        in an economy during
   a period of time         .

 Three alternative measurement approaches are
   adopted by the Statistician (the Australian
   Bureau of Statistics) in calculating GDP:
    –  Expenditure (E)
    –  Income (I)
    –  Production (P)

(1) THE EXPENDITURE APPROACH TO CALCULATING GDP

  Adds up all expenditure on final output:

    –   household consumption expenditure
    –   private investment expenditure
    –  government consumption expenditure
    –  exports    less imports      (net exports)
    –  changes in stocks




                    GDP = C          +  I +G     + ( X –     M   )

  (2) THE INCOME APPROACH TO CALCULATING
                                    GDP

 GDP consists of the sum of the incomes derived
by the factors of production from  producing the
GDP, namely:


     – the compensation of employees (wages, salaries and
       supplements)       –  the largest component

     – the gross operating surpluses (profits) of businesses
       and governments

     – indirect taxes less subsidies (which must be added to
       derive expenditure on GDP).











     (3) THE PRODUCTION OR VALUE ADDED
             METHOD IN CALCULATING GDP



             The sum of the value of all goods and services
             produced by industries in the economy in a year
             minus the costs of goods and services used in
             production –     leaving the value added.

Shortcomings of GDP as a measure of wellbeing:


•The distribution of GDP is not captured in GDP measures.

•The value of leisure is not included in GDP.

•The level, quality of and access to health care is not measured in GDP.

•GDP is not adjusted for pollution or other negative effects of pollution.

•GDP is not adjusted for changes in crime and other social problems.

•Does not capture improvements in product quality.

    INFLATING  AND  DEFLATING GDP

There are two indices that can be used to make the
   price adjustment to money GDP:

•  The Consumer Price Index
    –  This measures the price level of a ‘market basket’ of
       goods and services for a typical family in the capital
       cities (it is published quarterly by the ABS)

•  The Implicit Price Deflator
    –  This measures the average level of price changes of C,
       I, G and Net export expenditure and is derived from the
       quarterly National Accounts data.











                         Measuring nominal and real GDP

     Measuring nominal and real GDP

            1.  The “output” measure of GDP
                 2.Nominal and real GDP

    Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is defined as
    the market value          of all the final goods and
    services     produced within an economy in a given
    period of time.









                                                                             16

              The “output” measure:  GDP                                O

•  Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is what an economy
   produces in total. The output measure of GDP (GDP                             O) is
   obtained by adding up the values, in dollar terms, of the
   outputs of all firms in the economy.


•  Only newly produced goods are counted: goods
   produced in previous years do not count.


•  Only final goods are counted:                 intermediate goods            used
   to produce final goods do not count. Why?








                                Nominal GDP
NominalGDPmeasuresthetotalvalueofgoods&servicesin
agivenperiod,intermsofthat                 period’s    dollars.Forexample:
             in 2003/04 Australia’s GDP was                $838 billion
             in 2004/05 Australia’s GDP was                $894 billion

    BUT does this mean Australia produced a greater services in 2004/05 relative to 2003/04?quantityof goods &


      Not necessarily: because these figures capture 2 things

               changes in the                    changes in       prices
             quantity     of output              paid for the output

           change in      standard                change in     costof
                   of living                              living

                                this is what we really want to know                 18

                 Nominal and real GDP

 •   Nominal GDP is calculated on the basis of current
                                prices:
                            For example
           Nominal GDP2005        = Quantity    2005 x Price2005

  •  Real GDP      is based on prices which applied in the
                             "base" year:

            Real GDP2005     = Quantity    2005 x PriceBaseYear










            Deflating GDP: Price Indexes

 Conceptually, real GDP is nominal GDP that has been
 “deflated    ” to strip out the effects of price rises.

 To deflate nominal GDP, we use the relevant price
 index, just like we would deflate any nominal quantity.

                  ____________Nominal GDPx 100
Real GDP =          Price Index


  Which price index should be used?
        • The   GDP deflator     is the best price index for this
                                   purpose.

    Illustrative example: nominal and real GDP


                            2014 2015 2016


  Nominal GDP                595 606 690
  in current
  prices ($b)
  Price index                 97 100 105
  (2015=100)

  Real GDP in         (595/97)*100                 ? ?
  2015 pr. ($b)

  21










The GDP Deflator

Measures the average level of prices of all goods and services that make
up GDP.

The GDP Deflator represents the rising prices component between
nominal and real GDP.

A measure of the price level calculated by dividing nominal GDP by
real GDP and multiplying by 100.



 GDP Deflator  =   Nominal GDP        x  100
                       Real GDP

 Two key factors determine labourproductivity (and output):
              1.   Increases in capital per hour worked                .
    ▪  Capital:   Manufactured goods that are used to produce other goods
         and services; examples include computers, office buildings and
                                         machines.
        ▪  Human capital:      The accumulated knowledge and skills that
           workers acquire from education and training or from their life
                                       experiences.
2.   Technological change:               This is change in the ability of a
        firm to produce output with a given quantity of inputs.

   ▪   Accumulating more inputs such as labour, capital, and raw materials
          will not ensure that an economy experiences economic growth
                       unless technological change also occurs.








What determines how fast economies grow?


   Which is more important for economic growth: More
                  capital or technological change?

▪  Technological change helps economies avoid diminishing
                                returns to capital.
                   ▪  Technological change includes:
      –  The replacement of existing capital with more productive
                                          capital.
                –  Reorganisinghow production takes place.

         The key to sustaining economic growth




   ▪   Technological change shifts the per-worker production
                                     function upwards.
  ▪   In the long run, a country will experience an increasing
           standard of living only if it experiences continuing
                                  technological change.
